cards.text: In a {{c1::container class}}, one class provides a container to hold multiple objects of {{c2::another type}}.

cards.front: What are the two main types of container classes?
cards.back:
Value containers and reference containers.
Value containers are compositions that store copies.
Reference containers are aggregations that store pointers or references.

cards.text: A {{c1::value container}} is a composition that stores {{c2::copies}} of the objects it is holding.
A {{c1::reference container}} is an aggregation that stores {{c2::pointers or references}} to objects that live {{c3::outside the container}}.

cards.front: Which type of container owns the objects it contains?
cards.back: A value container owns the objects (composition). When the container is destroyed, the objects are destroyed. A reference container does not own the objects (aggregation).

cards.question: What type of container class is this?
cards.explanation: This is a value container (composition). The IntArray owns the int array and manages its lifetime completely - it allocates in the constructor and deletes in the destructor.

cards.question: What type of container class is this?
cards.explanation: This is a reference container (aggregation). The PlayerGroup stores pointers to Players but does not manage their lifetime. The Players exist outside the container.

cards.front: What are some examples of container classes in the C++ standard library?
cards.back: std::vector, std::array, std::map, std::set, std::list, std::deque, and others from the Standard Template Library (STL).

cards.front: When should you use a value container vs a reference container?
cards.back: Use a value container when the container should own and manage the objects' lifetimes. Use a reference container when objects need to be shared, exist independently, or their lifetime is managed elsewhere.

cards.explanation:
Container classes typically provide:
  • Creation/insertion of elements
  • Removal/deletion of elements
  • Access to elements (by index, iterator, etc.)
  • Reporting number of elements
  • Memory management (for value containers)
  • Resizing/growing as needed

cards.text: A value container implements a {{c1::composition}} relationship, while a reference container implements an {{c2::aggregation}} relationship.

cards.front: What happens to the contained objects when a value container goes out of scope?
cards.back: The contained objects are destroyed because the value container owns and manages their lifetime (composition relationship).

cards.text: A value container is a {{c1::composition}} that stores {{c2::copies}}, while a reference container is an {{c3::aggregation}} that stores {{c4::pointers or references}}.
